text: The robotic system is docked to a 25mm multi-channel trocar, which contains a 3D camera and three working arms, positioned within the operative field.
The surgeon inserts the 3D camera into the trocar, followed by the insertion of three robotic working arms, preparing the robotic system for the procedure.
The robotic system recognizes the loaded instrument, which is then advanced into the operative field through the single-port setup.
The SP trocar is inserted through a transanal access platform, positioning the robotic instrument within the operative field.
The robotic system demonstrates full wrist and elbow articulation of the three working arms as the surgeon identifies the lesion within the operative field.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to mark the lesion, visible within the operative field.
The surgeon prepares to excise the lesion in a full thickness fashion, using robotic instruments visible in the operative field.
A holographic image displays the position of the camera and three robotic working arms at the six o'clock position within the operative field.
The video shows a three-dimensional view of the operative field, highlighting the spatial arrangement and articulation of the surgical instruments.
The surgeon enters the plane between the muscularis propria and the perirectal fat, carefully dissecting these tissue layers.
The mesorectum is visibly left intact during the procedure, preserving the tissue for potential radical resection if invasive cancer is detected.
The surgeon uses finely articulated instruments to orient the scissors correctly within the operative field, ensuring precise cutting along the intended plane.
A blood vessel is visible entering through the muscularis propria into the perirectal fat, demonstrating its anatomical course within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection in a full thickness manner around the entire circumference of the lesion, maintaining precision in the operative field.
The video displays the distinct layers of the rectal wall, including the mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, and perirectal fat, clearly visible during the surgical procedure.
The inner circular and outer longitudinal layers of the muscularis propria are clearly visible.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument for retraction around the lesion, demonstrating its capability for coagulation if needed.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to transect the residual blood supply entering the disc excision site, completing the dissection.
The surgical field shows a defect in the rectal wall, which appears hemostatic, indicating no active bleeding.
Following irrigation with a dilute betadine solution, an assistant uses a port to introduce a 3-0 Vicryl suture into the operative field.
The surgeon uses a third robotic arm to retract tissue, enhancing visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an instrument tie using the SP robotic arms, demonstrating the ease of manipulation and precision with the robotic system.
The surgeon closes the defect using a running full thickness suture, with full thickness bites clearly visible. This technique is used to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon manages a long length of suture endoluminally, which eliminates the need for multiple sutures during the procedure.
Two hands tighten the suture while a third robotic arm retracts tissue for optimal visualization. The video fast forwards to the completion and tying of the final knot in the suture.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the suture, marking the completion of the operation.
